action.memory_update: Attempting to resolve seed paper 'Attention is All You Need'

paper.title: Attention is All you Need

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

tldr.text: A new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely is proposed, which generalizes well to other tasks by applying it successfully to English constituency parsing both with large and limited training data.

paper.abstract: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks in an encoder-decoder configuration. The best performing models also connect the encoder and decoder through an attention mechanism. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely. Experiments on two machine translation tasks show these models to be superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train. Our model achieves 28.4 BLEU on the WMT 2014 English-to-German translation task, improving over the existing best results, including ensembles by over 2 BLEU. On the WMT 2014 English-to-French translation task, our model establishes a new single-model state-of-the-art BLEU score of 41.8 after training for 3.5 days on eight GPUs, a small fraction of the training costs of the best models from the literature. We show that the Transformer generalizes well to other tasks by applying it successfully to English constituency parsing both with large and limited training data.

Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use."}, "fieldsOfStudy": ["Computer Science"], "s2FieldsOfStudy": [{"category": "Computer Science", "source": "external"}, {"category": "Computer Science", "source": "s2-fos-model"}], "tldr": {"model": "tldr@v2.0.0", "text": "Text-Guided Attention for Zero-Shot Robustness (TGA-ZSR) is proposed, which combines the text-guided attention obtained from the target model via adversarial examples with the text-guided attention acquired from the original model via clean examples to enhance the model's robustness."}, "publicationTypes": ["JournalArticle"], "publicationDate": "2024-10-29", "authors": [{"authorId": "2280197904", "name": "Lu Yu"}, {"authorId": "2315445302", "name": "Haiyang Zhang"}, {"authorId": "2328091704", "name": "Changsheng Xu"}], "abstract": "Due to the impressive zero-shot capabilities, pre-trained vision-language models (e.g. CLIP), have attracted widespread attention and adoption across various domains. Nonetheless, CLIP has been observed to be susceptible to adversarial examples. Through experimental analysis, we have observed a phenomenon wherein adversarial perturbations induce shifts in text-guided attention. Building upon this observation, we propose a simple yet effective strategy: Text-Guided Attention for Zero-Shot Robustness (TGA-ZSR). This framework incorporates two components: the Attention Refinement module and the Attention-based Model Constraint module. Our goal is to maintain the generalization of the CLIP model and enhance its adversarial robustness: The Attention Refinement module aligns the text-guided attention obtained from the target model via adversarial examples with the text-guided attention acquired from the original model via clean examples. This alignment enhances the model's robustness. Additionally, the Attention-based Model Constraint module acquires text-guided attention from both the target and original models using clean examples. Its objective is to maintain model performance on clean samples while enhancing overall robustness. The experiments validate that our method yields a 9.58% enhancement in zero-shot robust accuracy over the current state-of-the-art techniques across 16 datasets. Our code is available at https://github.com/zhyblue424/TGA-ZSR."}, {"paperId": "406705ffa93d1ae8a43c458b0a4fc02777b785b9", "externalIds": {"DBLP": "journals/corr/abs-2510-19861", "ArXiv": "2510.19861", "DOI": "10.48550/arXiv.2510.19861", "CorpusId": 282304543}, "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/406705ffa93d1ae8a43c458b0a4fc02777b785b9", "title": "Some Attention is All You Need for Retrieval", "venue": "arXiv.org", "year": 2025, "referenceCount": 12, "citationCount": 0, "influentialCitationCount": 0, "openAccessPdf": {"url": "", "status": null, "license": null, "disclaimer": "Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.19861, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use."}, "fieldsOfStudy": ["Computer Science"], "s2FieldsOfStudy": [{"category": "Computer Science", "source": "external"}, {"category": "Psychology", "source": "s2-fos-model"}], "tldr": null, "publicationTypes": ["JournalArticle"], "publicationDate": "2025-10-21", "authors": [{"authorId": "2356624786", "name": "Felix Michalak"}, {"authorId": "2356624280", "name": "Steven Abreu"}], "abstract": "We demonstrate complete functional segregation in hybrid SSM-Transformer architectures: retrieval depends exclusively on self-attention layers. Across RecurrentGemma-2B/9B and Jamba-Mini-1.6, attention ablation causes catastrophic retrieval failure (0% accuracy), while SSM layers show no compensatory mechanisms even with improved prompting. Conversely, sparsifying attention to just 15% of heads maintains near-perfect retrieval while preserving 84% MMLU performance, suggesting self-attention specializes primarily for retrieval tasks. We identify precise mechanistic requirements for retrieval: needle tokens must be exposed during generation and sufficient context must be available during prefill or generation. This strict functional specialization challenges assumptions about redundancy in hybrid architectures and suggests these models operate as specialized modules rather than integrated systems, with immediate implications for architecture optimization and interpretability."}, {"paperId": "5f0085e27a213a1f45f68525ce4da816a5362f1d", "externalIds": {"DBLP": "journals/corr/abs-2512-11254", "ArXiv": "2512.11254", "DOI": "10.48550/arXiv.2512.11254", "CorpusId": 283883677}, "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/5f0085e27a213a1f45f68525ce4da816a5362f1d", "title": "FAIR: Focused Attention Is All You Need for Generative Recommendation", "venue": "arXiv.org", "year": 2025, "referenceCount": 35, "citationCount": 0, "influentialCitationCount": 0, "openAccessPdf": {"url": "", "status": null, "license": null, "disclaimer": "Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.11254, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use."}, "fieldsOfStudy": ["Computer Science"], "s2FieldsOfStudy": [{"category": "Computer Science", "source": "external"}, {"category": "Computer Science", "source": "s2-fos-model"}], "tldr": {"model": "tldr@v2.0.0", "text": "FAIR is proposed, the first generative recommendation framework with focused attention, which enhances attention scores to relevant context while suppressing those to irrelevant ones, and is validated on four public benchmarks, demonstrating its superior performance compared to existing methods."}, "publicationTypes": ["JournalArticle"], "publicationDate": "2025-12-12", "authors": [{"authorId": "2344979746", "name": "Longtao Xiao"}, {"authorId": "2315579677", "name": "Haolin Zhang"}, {"authorId": "24140498", "name": "Guohao Cai"}, {"authorId": "2240695630", "name": "Jieming Zhu"}, {"authorId": "2345326379", "name": "Yifan Wang"}, {"authorId": "2371085958", "name": "Heng Chang"}, {"authorId": "2274021958", "name": "Zhenhua Dong"}, {"authorId": "2297866189", "name": "Xiu Li"}, {"authorId": "2260298889", "name": "Ruixuan Li"}], "abstract": "Recently, transformer-based generative recommendation has garnered significant attention for user behavior modeling. However, it often requires discretizing items into multi-code representations (e.g., typically four code tokens or more), which sharply increases the length of the original item sequence. This expansion poses challenges to transformer-based models for modeling user behavior sequences with inherent noises, since they tend to overallocate attention to irrelevant or noisy context. To mitigate this issue, we propose FAIR, the first generative recommendation framework with focused attention, which enhances attention scores to relevant context while suppressing those to irrelevant ones. Specifically, we propose (1) a focused attention mechanism integrated into the standard Transformer, which learns two separate sets of Q and K attention weights and computes their difference as the final attention scores to eliminate attention noise while focusing on relevant contexts; (2) a noise-robustness objective, which encourages the model to maintain stable attention patterns under stochastic perturbations, preventing undesirable shifts toward irrelevant context due to noise; and (3) a mutual information maximization objective, which guides the model to identify contexts that are most informative for next-item prediction. We validate the effectiveness of FAIR on four public benchmarks, demonstrating its superior performance compared to existing methods."}, {"paperId": "fe75d7c67788f39803ae39842d3b45bc5c34ff30", "externalIds": {"DBLP": "journals/corr/abs-2507-00537", "ArXiv": "2507.00537", "DOI": "10.48550/arXiv.2507.00537", "CorpusId": 280149643}, "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/fe75d7c67788f39803ae39842d3b45bc5c34ff30", "title": "Not All Attention Heads Are What You Need: Refining CLIP's Image Representation with Attention Ablation", "venue": "arXiv.org", "year": 2025, "referenceCount": 40, "citationCount": 1, "influentialCitationCount": 0, "openAccessPdf": {"url": "", "status": null, "license": null, "disclaimer": "Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.00537, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use."}, "fieldsOfStudy": ["Computer Science"], "s2FieldsOfStudy": [{"category": "Computer Science", "source": "external"}, {"category": "Computer Science", "source": "s2-fos-model"}], "tldr": {"model": "tldr@v2.0.0", "text": "Results highlight that AAT can effectively refine large-scale VLMs with virtually no extra inference cost, while yielding semantically meaningful patterns that align with existing interpretability findings."}, "publicationTypes": ["JournalArticle"], "publicationDate": "2025-07-01", "authors": [{"authorId": "2280850617", "name": "Feng Lin"}, {"authorId": "2372304817", "name": "Marco Chen"}, {"authorId": "2316668799", "name": "Haokui Zhang"}, {"authorId": "2281003213", "name": "Xiaotian Yu"}, {"authorId": "2263081813", "name": "Guangming Lu"}, {"authorId": "2280334058", "name": "Rong Xiao"}], "abstract": "This paper investigates the role of attention heads in CLIP's image encoder. Building on interpretability studies, we conduct an exhaustive analysis and find that certain heads, distributed across layers, are detrimental to the resulting representations. To mitigate their impact, we propose a simple yet effective Attention Ablation Technique (AAT) that suppresses selected heads by directly manipulating their attention weights. By incorporating two complementary strategies tailored to different application scenarios, AAT enables the systematic identification and ablation of harmful heads with minimal overhead. Experiments show that AAT consistently improves downstream performance across diverse domains, boosting recall by up to 11.1% on cross-modal retrieval benchmarks. These results highlight that AAT can effectively refine large-scale VLMs with virtually no extra inference cost, while yielding semantically meaningful patterns that align with existing interpretability findings."}, {"paperId": "6bac4b800578bbfdeff9ee7dde3cd1a846cafb0e", "externalIds": {"ArXiv": "2401.11143", "CorpusId": 267068511}, "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/6bac4b800578bbfdeff9ee7dde3cd1a846cafb0e", "title": "Density Adaptive Attention is All You Need: Robust Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning Across Multiple Modalities", "venue": "", "year": 2024, "referenceCount": 49, "citationCount": 5, "influentialCitationCount": 0, "openAccessPdf": {"url": "", "status": null, "license": null, "disclaimer": "Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.11143, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. arguments.heading: ## Closest Related Work (Ablation/Robustness)
arguments.content:
- **ID**: fe75d7c67788f39803ae39842d3b45bc5c34ff30
- **Title**: Not All Attention Heads Are What You Need: Refining CLIP's Image Representation with Attention Ablation
- **Year**: 2025
- **Source**: arXiv:2507.00537
- **Note**: Uses attention ablation to identify and suppress harmful heads in CLIP, improving cross-modal retrieval. Supports the idea that not all attention components are beneficial or equivalent.
